coredata.dc:title: Design and characterization of a novel pH-sensitive biocompatible and multifunctional nanocarrier for in vitro paclitaxel release
coredata.dc:creator: Vajedi, Fahimeh Sadat
Dehghani, Hossein
Zarrabi, Ali
coredata.dc:description: Breast cancer is one of the main reasons of women's mortality. A novel ternary combination of ZnAl-layered double hydroxides (LDH), cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) and N-graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) proposes a pH-sensitive multifunctional nanocomposite that can improve therapeutic features of each compound; this is a notable strategy to make biocompatible materials with unique properties for paclitaxel (PTX) delivery in breast cancer cells. For proving the surface modification process of materials, electrochemical techniques including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were carried out. By coating PEG on the surface of the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH, it developed a drug delivery system with low toxicity, an excellent encapsulation efficiency 88.4%, drug loading capacity of ca. 31%, and slow and sustained release behavior (9% after 72 h) under normal physiological conditions. Besides, a high drug release (~69%) at low-pH as a model of the extracellular tumor environment indicated a pH-sensitive release behavior. Moreover, cell viability assay proved the negligible cytotoxicity on normal cells (L929) and the improved growth inhibition effect of PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarrier on MCF7 cancer cells. Blood compatibility test values with respect to red blood cell aggregation (RBC), coagulation prothrombin time (PT), activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), and complement activation (C3 and C4 levels) remained within normal ranges without toxicity effect on RBCs and complement factors. Overall, this novel designed PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarrier with tremendously biocompatible, slow-release and pH-dependent features could be considered as a theranostic candidate for various anticancer drugs delivery and cancer therapy.

head.ce:title: Design and characterization of a novel pH-sensitive biocompatible and multifunctional nanocarrier for in vitro paclitaxel release

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Kashan, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Area Cod Box 87317-51167, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Breast cancer is one of the main reasons of women's mortality. A novel ternary combination of ZnAl-layered double hydroxides (LDH), cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) and N-graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) proposes a pH-sensitive multifunctional nanocomposite that can improve therapeutic features of each compound; this is a notable strategy to make biocompatible materials with unique properties for paclitaxel (PTX) delivery in breast cancer cells. For proving the surface modification process of materials, electrochemical techniques including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were carried out. By coating PEG on the surface of the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH, it developed a drug delivery system with low toxicity, an excellent encapsulation efficiency 88.4%, drug loading capacity of ca. 31%, and slow and sustained release behavior (9% after 72 h) under normal physiological conditions. Besides, a high drug release (~69%) at low-pH as a model of the extracellular tumor environment indicated a pH-sensitive release behavior. Moreover, cell viability assay proved the negligible cytotoxicity on normal cells (L929) and the improved growth inhibition effect of PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarrier on MCF7 cancer cells. Blood compatibility test values with respect to red blood cell aggregation (RBC), coagulation prothrombin time (PT), activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), and complement activation (C3 and C4 levels) remained within normal ranges without toxicity effect on RBCs and complement factors. Overall, this novel designed PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarrier with tremendously biocompatible, slow-release and pH-dependent features could be considered as a theranostic candidate for various anticancer drugs delivery and cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: A nature first-line chemotherapeutic drug for the cancer clinical treatments, especially breast cancer that is injected intravenously in the clinical therapies, is Paclitaxel (PTX). It is administered in the form of Taxol composed of ethanol and Cremophor EL as co-solvent [1]. However, its therapeutic effects are restricted due to the poor aqueous solubility, uncontrolled release rate, short half-life in blood circulation, the problem of drug resistance and undesirable side effects including nephrotoxicity, hypersensitivity, hyperlipidemia and neurotoxicity [2,3]. To improve the aqueous solubility of these hydrophobic drugs and to reduce their toxicity, various approaches were employed in the past few decades, such as the utilization of nanoparticles [4], liposomes [5] and other novel approaches. Among the effective approaches to solve the above problems in order to change its pharmacokinetic behavior, improve anticancer efficacy and reduce therapy doses is to encapsulate paclitaxel into nanocarriers through the host–guest interactions. Hence, it is essential for nanocarriers to have a series of proper features such as small size, biocompatibility, charge and surface functionality, water-solubility, high loading capacities and their removal from the biological system to develop an effective nanocarrier system.

A new class of inorganic nanocarriers, named as the layered double hydroxides (LDH), have received extensive attention as influential drug delivery systems because of their negligible toxicity, biocompatibility, easy preparation, unique intercalation, wide availability, pH-controlled release of guest molecules and high reserving capacity properties [6,7]. The structure of LDH consisted by brucite-like sheets of divalent and trivalent metal hydroxides with positive charge excess that was balanced by interlayer anions. Owing to the anionic exchange between LDH layers, the wide variety of biological molecules could act as interlayer anions, including DNA [8], vitamins [9], organic polymers and drugs [10] as anti-inflammatory or antineoplastics through either ion-exchange or coprecipitation. It is worth noting that interlayer anions substitute through electrostatic attraction, hydrogen-bonding and van der Waals interactions into the interlayer space. Wen's group [11], reported the intercalation of drugs such as DOX and PTX into the LDH matrix through electrostatic interaction. The multifunctional carboxymethyl chitosan derivatives-LDH hybrid nanocomposites could be applied for the dexamethasone disodium phosphate drug delivery to the posterior segment of the eye [12]. Salguero et al. reported the interaction Ciprofloxacin (Cip) anticancerous drug with ZnAl layered double hydroxide [13]. The LDH-Cip showed antibacterial activity for applications in skin wounds. The results showed that drug-LDH-based systems are a proper candidate to get over drug resistance and improve cancer therapy [14,15]. Nevertheless, the use of LDH nanosheets in vivo has been strictly confined their application due to lack of selective behavior towards the targeted sites, hard separation from aqueous solutions because of their small size, the rapid aggregation in culture solution or phosphate buffer saline (PBS), poor solubility in water, secure destruction in acidic environments, the poor ability to modify the surface, and low thermal stability [16,17]. These limitations can prompt to surface modification through interaction with a positive charge or its surface hydroxyl groups by targeting materials such as magnetic nanoparticles, showing promising properties in drug delivery [18,19]. The integration of metal oxides with LDH can reduce the bursting effect of drug release and prolong the release time of the drug [20].

Magnetic nanocarriers have gained much attention among many types of drug-carrying systems. Among these magnetic nanoparticles, cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) represents special applications in the fields of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), magnetic hyperthermia, drug delivery and biosensors, due to their chemical stability, high magnetic anisotropy, hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, large surface area to volume ratio and high electrochemical activity. These properties make spinel CoFe2O4 an attractive candidate to deliver drug to target tumor sites using an external magnet without interacting with the normal cells. In addition, the LDH nanosheets can be easily separated and collected by a magnetic field from aqueous solutions. The preparation of Fe3O4@LDH nanocomposite via delamination–reassembling process for delivery of enrofloxacin was reported by Li et al. [21]. Despite the positive effects of magnetic nanoparticles on LDH nanosheets, these nanoparticles reduce their stability due to the magnetic interaction of inter particles [22]. Additionally, the CoFe2O4 nanoparticles must be coated to avoid agglomeration and precipitate in the reticule-endothelial system before being present in the target tissues because the saturation magnetization and bonding capacity are reduced by accumulation [23,24]. It has also been reported that the CoFe2O4 nanoparticles without any surface coating have significant toxicity in vivo environments which results in reducing their efficiency and biomedical applications [25]. Therefore, surface modification of CoFe2O4 with various types of inorganic or organic substances becomes essential for diminishing these shortcomings for drug delivery systems. For this purpose, N-graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have been applied to keep them from accumulation and attain better anti-cancer efficiency [26].

Similar to graphene nanosheets, N-GQDs as a new class of zero-dimensional graphene nanomaterial, have attracted extensive considerations in drug delivery systems because of their inherent properties, such as electronic characteristics, low toxicity, chemical inertness, biocompatibility, simple functionalization, easy preparation, easy tunability of size and shape, easy dispersion in physiological environments and surface functionalization by using the π–π conjugated network [27]. So, N-GQDs as a potential ideal constituents to promote drug delivery properties of CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite can incorporate through interaction between nitrogen-containing functional groups of N-GQDs and the oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyl, epoxy, and carboxyl groups) on the surfaces of the layers [28]. Recently, Wang et al. have reported that the prepared GQDs play an important role in reducing the cytotoxic effects of DOX on cancer cells compared with free DOX [29]. The covalent linkage of N-GQDs onto the surface of CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite not only avoid aggregation or sedimentation but also provides an ideal nanocarrier with more active site for drug loading, simultaneous exploring and treatment of cancer cells. On the other hand, after loading PTX and N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH via π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, low cytotoxicity was observed in breast cancer cells. Coating the surface of nanocomposite with polyethylene glycol (PEG), which is a biocompatible super-hydrophilic polymer can provide enhanced permanence, and functional groups for the interconnection of drugs with great functional groups [30]. It can be covalently anchored onto N-GQDs, or adsorbed by electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bond, due to the abundant amino and carboxyl groups on the surfaces of the N-CQDs, resulting in reducing its toxicity, prolonging the circulation time of nanocomposite in the blood, higher surface area for improved loading and selective release, enhancing the anti-cancer drug performance and reducing side effects to normal cells [31].

Up to now, the development of a nanocarrier with a higher drug loading capacity, prolonged and sustained release and lower toxicity has become a hot topic of research for PTX delivery. In addition, many drug delivery systems faced with drug transport problems and nanocarrier toxicity. Besides, because of being acidic the external environment of the tumors than normal tissues, the formation of a pH-responsive carrier can lead to the control of the site-specific delivery of anticancer drugs, faster drug release in the tumor tissues without external stimulus and reduce the side effects of anticancer drugs to normal cells [32]. The combination of three or more nanoplatforms in one system for cancer treatment is very interesting due to their improved biodistribution, decreased toxicity, and side effects via the increased permeability and retention (EPR) effect [33,34]. So, due to the unique therapeutic effects for each of the mentioned compounds, the incorporation of three or more compounds in drug delivery systems is very interesting for enhancing the chemotherapy effects [35,36]. Utilizing stimuli-responsive drug delivery systems has become one of the important modality for enhancing effects of chemotherapy which are able to release drug with control of the area, time, and dosage, leading to increase the therapeutic efficiency along with a reduction in the side effects of the drugs by sensing and responding to external stimuli (e.g., temperature, pH, electric or magnetic field, ultrasound, light, etc.) [37,38]. Herein, we wish to report a new nanocarrier as a pH-responsive anti-cancer drug delivery system for PTX with integration of LDH nanosheets with nanoparticles CoFe2O4 and N-GQDs, presenting unique properties such as high drug loading, good dispersity, low toxicity, strong superparamagnetism and controlled drug delivery.

In addition to the drug slow release in physiological environments, this nanocomposite could significantly alleviate the drug resistance problem owing to its smart pH-dependent release behavior, which decreases the dosage of drug needed to be effective, and thus decreasing the drug resistance, as well. It should be noted that, MgAl-LDH/Fe2O3/graphene composites already were reported for the Removal of Pb(II) and 2,4- Dichlorophenoxyacetic Acid [39] and arsenate [40] from aqueous solutions. To the best of our knowledge, N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite is synthesized for the first time for the use and investigation as a nanocarrier for drug delivery, especially paclitaxel delivery, demonstrating the higher encapsulation than the previous studies [41–43]. Besides, there have been just previous reports of drug binding to graphene [44–46] and magnetic nanoparticles [47]. The biocompatibility of the nanocarrier (with and without drug) was assessed by cytotoxicity (MTT assay) and hemocompatibility (coagulation, complement activation, and hemolysis) tests and so far, there have been no reports to study the interaction of PTX-loaded nanocarriers with blood and its constituents. It could be concluded that this nanocomposite with high loading efficiency, lower toxicity and excellent blood compatibility could be appropriate for biological applications.

All chemicals used including ZnCl2·6H2O (Merck), AlCl3.6H2O (Merck), FeCl3.6H2O (98% Merck), CoCl2.6H2O (98% Merck), sodium oxalate (Merck), ethylene glycol (Merck), NaOH (>98% Merck), citric acid (CA) (Merck), propylamine (PA) (Merck), polyethylene glycol-6000 N (PEG), Paclitaxel (Shanghai Sunve Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd), dialysis membrane 12 kDa MWCO, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol- 2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) (Sigma), cell culture RPMI 1640 and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) (Bioldia), Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) (Gibco), penicillin–streptomycin (Bioldia), trypsin, ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (Bioldia), breast cancer cells (MCF-7) (Pasteur Institute of Iran), normal cells (L929) (Pasteur Institute of Iran), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (Merck) and ethanol (Merck) were of analytical purity and used as available. The aqueous solutions throughout the experiments were prepared daily in deionized distilled water (Millipore, Bedford, MA). The other reagents and solvents used in our experiments and solvents were all of the analytical grade.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using the KBr pellet technique (Magna 550 Nicolet spectrometer) to characterize functional groups existed in samples over the range 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The morphological structures and elemental composition of samples were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) pattern (TESCAN BRNO-Mira3 LMU). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (TEM EM208S PHILIPS) analysis operating at 100 kV was performed for the nanoparticles (NPs) characterization. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The size distribution and zeta potential of NPs were conducted on a Zeta potential analyzer (Microtrac Nano W3776) at ambient temperature. The phase structure properties of samples were determined by a XRD diffractometer (Phillips X'Pert PRO) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). Magnetic measurements were tested on a vibrating-sample magnetometer (VSM, JDAW-2000) operating in the temperature range 5–400 K. The cyclic voltammograms (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements (Sama 500 potentiostat (Isfahan, Iran)) were carried out using a three-electrode cell consisted of a modified glassy carbon (working), platinum wire (counter) and Ag/AgCl (reference) electrodes and 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis procedures for the breast cancer therapeutic studies
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite; The synthesis steps are as follows: (1) Synthesis of LDH nanosheets according to the coprecipitation method (2) Synthesis of CoFe2O4 NPs via a hydrothermal approach; (3) Synthesis of CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite; (4) Synthesis of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite by hydrothermal method and (5) PTX loading and in vitro studies release. The whole synthesis process is shown in Scheme 1 . The synthesis details are as follows;

A layered double hydroxide (LDH) were prepared according to the coprecipitation method. Briefly, a mixed salts solution containing approximately 10 mM of ZnCl2·6H2O and 5 mM of AlCl3·9H2O with a Zn2+/Al+3 M ratio of 2 and the second solution of NaOH (1 M, 100 mL) were simultaneously added to a beaker with vigorous stirring for 24 h. The final pH of the aqueous solution was adjusted to 8.5. The resulting suspension was kept under nitrogen flow to remove CO2. The white colloidal suspension was filtered, washed repeatedly via centrifugation with Milli-Q water until neutralizing pH and finally dried at 60 °C overnight.

A surfactant-supported hydrothermal method was employed for the synthesis of CoFe2O4 NPs. Firstly, stoichiometric proportions of FeCl3·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, ethylene glycol and sodium oxalate (in molar ratios of 2:1:3:2) were dissolved thoroughly in 20 mL deionized water, and stirred vigorously for 40 min to form a homogenous dispersion. Then, a solution of NaOH and NH4OH was dropped into the above solution with constant stirring for another 30 min to adjust its pH to 11 and a brown precipitate was formed. The obtained solution was sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave to be treated at 150 °C for 20 h. After filtration and washing steps, the precipitation of cobalt iron oxalate precursor was annealed at 500 °C for 3 h to make CoFe2O4 NPs.

To synthesize CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, first of all, a certain amount of CoFe2O4 dispersion was dissolved in an aqueous solution (25 mL) containing 0.5 mmol of ZnCl2.6H2O and 0.25 mmol of AlCl3·9H2O by vigorous stirring for 1 h. Following, 20 mL of mixed alkaline solution (1 M NaOH, 0.5 M Na2CO3) was added dropwise to the above mixed solution to adjust the final pH to 9.5 and was then continuously stirred for 1 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. The obtained slurry was heated at 80 °C for 12 h. The resulting black precipitates were isolated, rinsed thoroughly with Milli-Q water and ethanol several times, and consequently dried at 60 °C overnight.

A two-step process was performed for the preparation of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite. In the first procedure, N-GQDs were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and propylamine as the source materials. In brief, 10 mL of an aqueous solution containing CA (0.1 M) and PA (0.1 M) was kept under vigorous stirring at room temperature for 0.5 h to give a clear solution. Then, aqueous suspension was placed into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for about 5 h. The pH of the resulting solution was adjusted to 9 with NaOH (1 M) and was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the insoluble particles. The final yellow product was stored in a refrigerator. In the second procedure, N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite was prepared via a facile and efficient deposition. Simultaneously, 0.01 g of the as prepared LDH was added to 0.03 g CoFe2O4 in ethanol to form a 2 wt% homogeneous solution with ultrasonication for 2 h. After ultrasonication, the pH of the aqueous solution was maintained at 7. Then, the required amount of N-GQDs solution and PEG (10 M) was added to the mixed solution and was stirred vigorously for another 24 h at room temperature. Subsequently, the nanocomposite was detached by a permanent magnet, rinsed thoroughly with Milli-Q water, and dried at 60 °C [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loaded nanocarrier and in vitro release studies
ce:section.ce:para:
The loading and release profiles of a chemotherapeutic drug (PTX) onto the developed N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite were investigated. For PTX loading, the desired amount from each dispersed sample in ethanol (CoFe2O4 (0.03 g) and LDH (0.01 g)) was added to 10 mL N-GQDs solution and 0.1 g PEG. After ultrasonication for 15 min, followed by addition of 0.05 mL PTX (0.3 mg/mL) at pH 9 (with 1 M NaOH) and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. Then unbound excess PTX and precipitate were separated by a magnet, and the loaded amount of PTX in N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH was assessed by UV–vis spectroscopy at absorbance peak of 235 nm. The product was collected via centrifugation, then washed several times with ethanol and dried at room temperature. As a control, the LDH and CoFe2O4/LDH were synthesized with PTX drug under a similar condition. The percentage of drug loading capacity (DLC) and drug encapsulation efficiency (DEE) were calculated by the following two Eqs. (1) and (2):

The pH-dependent PTX release behavior from the LDH, CoFe2O4/LDH and N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH was investigated in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 7.4 and 5 by dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off =12,000 Da). Briefly, suitable amounts of drug-loaded carriers were dispersed in 10 mL of PBS. Then, 3 mL of solution were placed into dialysis bag and suspended in 25 mL of release medium (PBS, pH 7.4 and 5) at 37 °C under slight stirring. 2 mL of aliquots were extracted from the release medium at different times intervals (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 24, 48, and 72 h) and replaced with the equivalent amount of fresh buffer. The amount of released drug was determined by UV–vis spectroscopy at 235 nm wavelength. The percentage of drug release was calculated utilizing the Eq. 3:
(3)
Drug release
%
=
Drug release concentration Drug loading concentration
× 100

The electrochemical behavior of LDH, CoFe2O4/LDH, N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH and PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH was examined using cyclic voltammetry (CV). All CV experiments were conducted using a conventional three-electrode system, Ag/AgCl (reference), platinum wire, (counter) and glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (working electrodes) in phosphate buffer aqueous solution (PBS, pH 7.4) as the electrolyte. The surface of the GCE electrode was modified with 10 μL of the samples homogenous suspension and the CV measurements were performed at a scan rate of 10 mV/s in a −1 to 2 mV potential range.

In the present study, two types of cell lines, MCF-7 (breast cancer cells) and L929 (normal cell), were grown in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) complemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% streptomycin/penicillin in cell culture flasks under 5% CO2 at 37 °C. The cells were detached from culture plates by adding trypsin/EDTA solution after growing to more than 80% cellular confluence. After that, the solution of pristine drug, carriers with/without drug in medium culture with different concentrations of 200, 100, 50 and 25 μg/mL were further incubated for the duration of 72 h at 37 °C.

The cytotoxicity of prepared samples on two cell lines was assessed by standard MTT assay method, which is extensively utilized for the cell viability by measuring the reduction of the yellow tetrazolium dye to an insoluble formazan dye in mitochondria of alive cells. Briefly, the cells were cultured in a 96-well plate at the density of 5000 cells in each well. After 24 h incubation of cells, they were exposed to the pure PTX and PTX free samples and PTX loaded nanocarrier at various concentrations for 24, 48 and 72 h. After that time, the cells were washed by fresh PBS buffer, followed by replacing with 100 μL of fresh media containing 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL in PBS) which then incubated for a period of 4 h. Finally, MTT containing culture medium was discarded and 100 μL DMSO was added to wells and incubated for 20 min to completely dissolve the precipitated formazan in darkness with rocker. The absorbance of each solution was recorded at 570 nm by using a microplate Elisa reader (Bio-Rad, USA) and calculated by the following Eq. (4):
(4)
Cell viability
%
:
Abs sample
/
Abs control
× 100

Where Abs sample is the absorbance of the treated cells with different samples concentrations and Abs control is the absorbance of the cells with no samples added. Notable, each experiment was performed in triplicate.

To evaluate the effect of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH and PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite on blood coagulation time, activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) and prothrombin time (PT) were measured as parameters of blood compatibility for biomaterials using the blood coagulation analyzer (STAR Evolution, Diagnostica Stago, Assiernes, France). Herein, anti-coagulated plasma was added to the solution of nanocomposite with/without drug in isotonic saline (9:1 solution) with different concentrations of 25, 50, 75 and 100 μg mL−1 and mixtures were then incubated at 37 °C for 2 h before activating with coagulation factors. For control experiments, identical volumes of plasma were added to the saline solution without any nanocomposite. After incubation for 2 h at 37 °C, Innovin ® reagent and actin were added into the solution and the clotting time then measured to determine the PT and APTT, respectively. Experiments were repeated three times [49].

To assess complement activation, the cleavage of C3 and C4 component was characterized by monitoring through a commercial single radial immuno-diffusion (SRID) immunoassay kit. For this study plasma separated by centrifugation from the anti-coagulated blood samples (collected from healthy volunteer). Different concentrations of PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH (25, 50, 75, and 100 μg mL−1) and N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH (100 μg mL−1) in isotonic saline (1:1 solution) were added to the separated plasma. Furthermore, plasma and saline were mixed as the control sample without any nanocomposite. After incubation for 2 h at 37 °C the concentration of C3/C4 was measured and compared with the control to evaluate the effect of nanocomposite on activation of complement system [50].

Briefly, a fresh anti-coagulated blood sample (3 mL) collected from healthy volunteer (with ethical permissions) was centrifuged at 1600 rpm for 5 min to collect RBC cells. The collected cells were washed several times with PBS to obtain a clear supernatant and then 200 μL of washed RBC was entirely diluted with 3.8 mL of PBS. Various concentrations (25, 50, 75, and 100 μg mL−1) of PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH suspension in PBS (0.8 mL) was mixed with diluted RBC suspension (0.2 mL) and kept without shaking for 2 h at room temperature. Distilled water and PBS were used as positive and negative controls, respectively. After 2 h, the solution of each sample was centrifuged and the absorbance of the supernatant was detected at 541 nm by UV–visible spectrometry to determine the % of hemolysis. The degree of hemolysis (%) was calculated for each sample using the following Eq. (5):
(5)
Percent Hemolysis
%
:
sample absorbance − negative control absorbance
positive control absorbance − negative control absorbance
× 10

All quantitative data are calculated as mean values ± standard deviation (SD) and were analyzed using SPSS software (version 21). Statistical comparison test was considered remarkable at the value of less than 0.05 and were carried out using parametric analysis of variance [ANOVA (Tukey)] at least three times parallel repeated experiment.

The surface morphology of the as-prepared LDH, CoFe2O4/LDH, N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH, and N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH intercalated with PTX were further investigated by FE-SEM technique, as shown in Fig. 1 . It is observed in Fig. 1a, the FE-SEM image of the pristine LDH reveals the thin hexagonal nanoplatelets and non-porous with irregular edges. After assembly with small magnetite CoFe2O4 NPs, it is clearly seen that a large number of CoFe2O4 NPs which are composed of homogeneous spherical structure are randomly distributed on the surface of host honeycomb-like LDH nanosheets with some agglomeration. This CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite preserved the hexagonal structure as LDH nanosheets with open space among them (Fig. 1b). From the FE-SEM image of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite (Fig. 1c), it is evident that the strong electrostatic particle–particle interactions were performed between the positively charged LDH layer, the negatively charged CoFe2O4 surface and the positively charged N-GQDs, the particles distributed on the LDH surface, and the size and thickness of LDH layers changed, resulting in the formation of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite. It is noticeable that the introduced N-GQDs did not influence the shape of each sample and each of them is recognizable in this nanocomposite. After the intercalation of PTX in N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite (Fig. 1d), it can be seen that the presence of the biomolecules influences moderately the morphology of nanocomposite and because of the irregular plate–plate stacking the particles are closely and continuously aggregated compare to bare LDH. Similarly, a strong particle-particle interaction among all particles with PTX, confirming the successful attachment of drug into the nano-platform.

The EDS spectroscopy analysis for the prepared LDH nanosheets (Fig. 2a) and CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite (Fig. 2b) shows the presence of Fe, Co, Zn, Al, Cl and C elements without any impurities. Whereas, after mixing CoFe2O4/LDH with N-GQDs (Fig. 2c), the EDX analysis results confirmed high content of C, O and the presence of an additional peak of N element in the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite compared with the spectrum of CoFe2O4/LDH, gives the evidence that N-GQDs is well loaded on the surface of CoFe2O4/LDH. The EDS spectrum of PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarrier (Fig. 2d) indicates the C/O ratio is more than the expected value. It is noticeable that the samples are completely pure and there are no additional peaks related to contaminations or impurities.

Fig. 3 displays the TEM image of (a) LDH (b) CoFe2O4 (c) N-GQDs (d) N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH and (e) PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH also exhibited. As shown in Fig. 3a, image of LDH clearly showed hexagonal plate-like structures which were consistent with SEM analysis. Fig. 3b displays the spherical and uniform shape of CoFe2O4 with spinel structure and high purity. The microstructures of the as-prepared N-GQDs with high intensity of black dots were relatively uniform with a size distribution of 2–10 nm and well dispersed without agglomeration (Fig. 3c). It can be seen that in Fig. 3d and e, an abundant number of both the roughly spherical CoFe2O4 NPs and LDH planes with a narrow size dispersion has still preserved their structures and N-GQDs with high intensity of black dots successfully anchored onto the LDH surface indicating the formation of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite.

The particle size distributions and zeta potential were measured using DLS and are revealed in Fig. 4 . Small particles with high zeta potential (negative or positive) will indicate stability. As the zeta potential decreases, the electrostatic repulsion will exceed and lead to accumulation [51]. As shown in Fig. 4 . The surface of LDH is positively charged, confirmed by a zeta potential of +38 mV that can be effective for the further immobilizing on the negative surface of samples. From DLS measurements, it was found that the CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite has an average particle size of 289 nm, polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.183 and a negative zeta potential of −23.3 mV. Changing the surface potential from +38 to −23.3 indicated CoFe2O4 NPs anchored on the surface of the LDH nanosheets. Although with further modification with the NGQDs and PEG, a slight reduction in the average particle size near 171 nm, PDI of 0.111 and a negative zeta potential of −38.7 mV was found, a stable suspension was still achieved. N-GQDs and PEG hinder the agglomeration of LDH-CoFe2O4, reduces the interparticle interactions that lead to increase of the surface area and reduction in the average particle size of composite. In addition, a high zeta potential (negative or positive) for small particles indicates stability in the solution and resist aggregation [52,53]. A drastic decrease was observed by DLS analysis in the average particle size to ~121 nm, PDI of 0.0791 and a large negative value of zeta potential of −62 mV after PTX loading that this change in zeta potential confirms a stable relative nanocomposite with the well-dispersed state. It is important to note that size distribution for PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarrier without PEG (224 nm) was wider as compared to that of nanocarrier with PEG (121 nm). These results showed that the stability of PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarriers synthesized not only depends on the size of the N-GQDs or the amount of molecules loaded for functionalization, but also the type of molecule on the GQDs nanoparticles [52].

The purity and crystalline features of the LDH, CoFe2O4, CoFe2O4/LDH, N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH and PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH were characterized using XRD (Fig. 5 ). As can be clearly observed, the characteristic diffraction peaks of LDH/CoFe2O4 at 2θ = 30.48° (220), 35.86° (311), 37.13° (222), 43.5° (400), 53.96° (422), 57.46° (511), 62.97° (440) and 74.91° (533) can be matched well with the typical face-centered cubic (fcc) crystal structure model (JCPDS No.22-1086) for CoFe2O4 NPs, while 2θ = 11.55°, 23.14°, 34.64°, 39.19°, 46.63°, 52.65°, 55.93°, 60.22° and 61.53° correspond to the (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (1010), (0111), (110), and (113) planes of LDH nanosheets with a rhombohedral symmetry (JCPDS file No. 48-1021). All the main peaks of LDH and CoFe2O4 are well preserved in the CoFe2O4/LDH sample and no shift was observed [54]. It is known that all main diffraction peaks of the CoFe2O4/LDH sample in the XRD pattern for the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite with the reduced diffraction intensity are not distorted by the addition of some N-GQDs and no other strong diffraction peak is found. This partial reduction in intensity could be attributed to low content and fine dispersion of N-GQDs in the nanocomposites. Furthermore, it displays that the growth of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4on the LDH has no apparent effect on the phase structure of surface modified LDH. Moreover, for PTX-loaded nanocarrier, the same characteristic XRD peaks of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite could be observed after the incorporation of drug which are much strong compared to those in nanocomposite due to the greater immobilization of drug and strong interaction between drug and nanocomposite. The average crystallite size of the samples was calculated using the Scherrer's equation were 41 nm for PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH and 44 nm for N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH. These results confirm the successful preparation of PTX-loaded nanocarrier.

The FTIR spectra of the LDH, CoFe2O4, CoFe2O4/LDH, N-GQDs, N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH, PTX, and PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH were also performed for further investigation of samples. As is shown in Fig. 6 , the FT-IR spectrum of CoFe2O4/LDH sample indicates a combination of absorption bands features similar to LDH and CoFe2O4. Two absorption bands observed at about 3450 and 1630 cm−1 are attributed to the OH bond stretching vibration and water deformation and bending vibrations of the interlayer water molecules, respectively. A band at 1362 cm−1 indicates the presence of CO3 2− anions between the LDH layers. Additionally, the characteristic absorption bands in the lower wavenumber region from ~1000 to 400 cm−1 can be corresponded to the ZnO and AlO lattice vibrations in the octahedral hydroxyl sheets of LDH and the stretching vibrations of tetrahedral groups ([Fe3+–O2−] and ([Co2+-O2−] and the stretching of [Fe3+-O2−]) at the octahedral sites of the spinel ferrite [39,55,56]. The FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs exhibited the presence of functional groups on the surface of the prepared N-GQDs. As is seen, the broad absorption peak at 3575 cm−1 is related to OH/NH groups stretching vibration, while the characteristic absorption peaks at 2969, 2530 and 2056 cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CH, the carboxylic acid OH stretch and CN stretching vibration, respectively. The emerging absorption bands at around 1707 and 1637 cm−1 are assigned to the carboxyl acid groups (-COOH), and amide CONH, as well as the existence of typical bands at 1387, 1198 and 1043 cm−1 are mostly attributed to the stretching vibration modes of CNH, COH (alkoxy) and the CO (epoxy) in N-GQDs, respectively [57]. In addition, the characteristic peaks in the spectral region below 1000 cm−1 were ascribed to the plane bending vibration of NH. Those peaks indicate the presence of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs which suggest the N-GQDs have high hydrophilic and stability property in aqueous systems. After covalent binding PEG and N-GQDs with CoFe2O4/LDH, the same bands as CoFe2O4/LDH are observed with a slight red shift in the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH sample, although some vibrations overlapped. It could also be seen that the strong bands at 1707 and 1043 cm−1 from the N-GQDs entirely disappeared because of the chemical binding with the LDH and CoFe2O4, but the existence of two new characteristic bands at 2923 and 1115 cm−1 that is allocated to the asymmetric vibrations of CH and CN/COC stretching from both N-GQDs and PEG confirms that N-GQDs and PEG are coordinated with CoFe2O4/LDH. Notably, PEG molecules make to increase the solubility of the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH in cellular solutions for biological applications. For FTIR spectrum of PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarrier, not only all the characteristic peaks of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH are discernible in the PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH but also a few additional absorption peaks were appeared at 3240 cm−1 (NH), 2856 and 1422 cm−1C-H (alkanes), 1588 cm−1CC (aromatic rings), 1279 cm−1C-N/COC, 1074 cm−1CO/CH bonds and 843–755 cm−1 aromatic CH bending [54,58]. which are the typical absorption peaks of PTX, indicate the successful loading of PTX to N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH.

The magnetic nature of CoFe2O4 NPs, CoFe2O4/LDH and N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite was investigated with a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) technique at room temperature. Fig. 7 depicts the magnetic hysteresis loops (S-like curves (with an applied magnetic fields of −15 to 15 KOe indicating characteristic superparamagnetic behavior for all three samples with nearly zero remanent magnetization (Mr) and coercivity (Hc). It could be observed that the specific saturation magnetization of the as-synthesized CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite is estimated to be 29 emu g−1, less than the value of corresponding bulk CoFe2O4 (41 emu g−1), which can be attributed to the presence of diamagnetic component as LDH nanosheets and size-dependent magnetization. After conjugation with N-GQDs layers coating on the surface of CoFe2O4/LDH, a further reduction in Ms. value was observed for the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH compared to that for CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite, being 12.5 emu g−1 that is most likely due to mitigate of magnetic component in the nanocomposite (the existence of the nonmagnetic LDH and N-GQDs in the composite). Although the Ms. value of N-GQDs-decorated CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite is smaller with respect to that of the bulk CoFe2O4, but the its magnetic strength was extremely appropriate for the magnetic response to an external magnetic field. So, the strong magnetic sensitivity of the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH and CoFe2O4/LDH can provide possibility to easy separation of large biomolecules after being used for drug delivery to targeted positions using a magnetic separation process. These results are indirect evidence to confirm the chemical bonding interactions between N-GQDs, CoFe2O4, and LDH components in the CoFe2O4/LDH and N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposites.

The electrochemical performance of different samples modified GCE was studied by CV and DPV measurements in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) to confirm conjunction of PTX on the nanocomposite surface. As is shown in Fig. 8a, no new redox peak was observed after modification of bare GCE (curve a) with LDH nanosheets electrode, but peak current was higher in comparison to bare GCE (curve b), which may be due to the large surface area and accelerate the electron transfer of the LDH nanosheets. When CoFe2O4/LDH/GCE was alternatively considered, in addition to the increase in peak current, a pair of well-defined redox peak (curve c) was observed which could be assigned to the Co(ІІI)/Co (II) species redox process, demonstrating a reversible electron transfer system and pseudocapacitance behavior of CoFe2O4. Further, at the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH/GCE electrode (curve d), a dramatic increase in redox peak current associated with changing in the position of the potential peak to more negative values (from −0.19 to −0.54 and from 1.48 to 1.31) was found after the addition of N-GQDs to CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite. The reasons for improving the electrochemical behavior and facilitating electron transfer of this electrode can be the high conductivity, unique electronic structure and high surface area of N-GQDs. Upon binding of PTX to N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite (curve e), relative intensity and position of reduction and oxidation peaks changed under the same conditions. Additionally, the redox peak positions have shifted sharply to more negative values confirming the surface modification of materials and the effective conjugation of PTX with N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite [59].

Fig. 8b shows the obtained DPV results for the samples and displays that the current response of each electrode is the same as the increment trend in the current of electrodes measured by CV. Therefore, these results demonstrate successful modification of LDH and immobilization of PTX on the surface of the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anticancer drug loading (PTX) and release profile
ce:section.ce:para: The PTX release behavior in response to the pH changes was also investigated from different nanocarriers in PBS (pH 7.4) and acidic buffer (pH 5) as a pH model of blood and the extracellular tumor environment, respectively, at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 10 , a similar feature for all drug-loaded nanocarriers in both pH was nonlinear-release profile compared with free drug, characterized by the slight burst effect phenomenon in the early stages of drug release (the first 10 h), followed by slower and sustained release within 72 h of the study. The initial burst release was most likely because the release of the adsorbed drug molecules on and near the surface of the samples that can be useful for the cancer cells growth suppression at the early stage and lastly. Slow and sustained release of PTX inside samples may be corresponded to drug well-entrapment in samples. It was obvious that free PTX exhibited a shorter-term release behavior compared with drug-loaded nanocarriers for both pHs and had an 80% (pH 7.4) and 90% (pH 5) release in 12 h. Besides, the drug release rate at pH 7.4 (Fig. 10a) was much lower than that at pH 5 (Fig. 10b) for all nanocarriers. In fact, to diminish the negative side effects of the drug, the release of the drug into normal tissues must be zero or less than that in the cancerous tissue. These results are comparable to previous reports [41–47]. This behavior was mainly due to the high dissolution and diffusion rates of nanocarriers at low pH. As shown in Fig. 10a the detachment rate of the PTX from N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarrier during the initial stages (in the first 10 h incubation) was found to be no higher than 7.6% compared with the CoFe2O4/LDH (16%) and LDH (32%), this was probably owing to the minimal drug leakage from nanocarrier under simulated physiological conditions (pH 7.4) which minimizes adverse effects on normal tissues whereas a higher release at pH 5 was observed at the same time (approximately 15.6%) (Fig. 10b). However, it is clearly observed that PTX/LDH displayed a high release of PTX in both pH 5.0 (52%) and 7.4 (38%) after 48 h due to poor binding of the drug to the LDH. As expected, after conjugating of LDH with CoFe2O4 and N-GQDs, the cumulative release of PTX at both pHs decreased due to their operation as strong barriers for fast PTX release. The results of the PTX release from CoFe2O4/ZnAl-LDH and N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocarriers revealed that after 48 h about 27% and 16.5% of PTX was released at pH 7.4, respectively, while in the case of the pH 5, the PTX release was approximately doubled at the same time. Since after approximately 48 h, drug release did not reach a final plateau, release continued after 72 h studied. In the following 72 h, the PTX release percentage of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH, CoFe2O4/LDH and LDH is followed by a slow-release about 22%, 31% and 43% at pH 7.4, respectively which was increased up to 33.6%, 42.6 and 69 at pH 5 respectively. PTX drug loaded N-GODs/CoFe2O4/LDH increases the circulation time because of resistance to plasma protein deposition. It is worth noting that all the nanocarriers had the pH-sensitive drug released behavior that is desirable for targeted cancer treatment and complete drug release occurs much longer time than 72 h. It is clearly seen that the CoFe2O4/LDH coating with N-GQDs and PEG in the space between the drug and sample and strong attachment of drug due to the electrostatic interaction, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding could influence the release behavior of nanocarriers. Thus, the PTX release rate from N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH was slower than the other samples and the most amount of PTX will remain in the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite for a considerably long time period, resulting in good drug stability in the nanocarrier without any side effects by controlling for a long time. This nanocarrier was able to delay the burst release and allows for a continuous fight against cancer cells, reducing the viability of cancer cells as revealed in the section of in vitro cytotoxicity. In other words, the PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite can considerably regulating the release of the loaded drug that turn off in the bloodstream and “turn on” in the tumor environment. The pH-dependent release behavior is acceptable to help treat cancer (a slight acidic environment in the tumor stimulates the release of PTX into the target tissue to kill cancer cells competently).

In order to estimate the cytotoxicity effect of PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH and N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH, the in vitro MTT assay used as an indicator of live cells, mitochondrial function. In this test, L-929 and MCF-7 cell lines (as normal and cancer cells, respectively) were exposed with different concentrations of nanocomposite with/without drug components (25, 50, 75 and 100 μg/mL) for 24, 48, and 72 h and then the viability of the cells was distinguished by MTT colorimetric assay. Fig. 11 shows the results of cytotoxicity effect of N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH on both cell lines. Results confirmed that the samples had no significant cytotoxicity effect on the cells and the cell viability for all concentrations was much more than 85%, which can approve the biocompatibility of the nanocarrier. Besides, the viability of L-929 cell was noticeably more than MCF-7 cancer cells which is a fundamental characteristic for their bio-applications. It is suggested that the appropriate surface coating of the carriers with PEG make them biocompatible via protecting cells from directly exposed with the nanocomposite [44].

The cytotoxic effects of PTX-loaded nanocarrier on normal and cancer cell lines are depicted in Fig. 12 . The results of experiments revealed that L-929 treated with drug-loaded nanocarrier (Fig. 12a) shows no considerable cytotoxicity effect in low concentrations of nanocarriers while by increasing the concentrations (>100 μg/mL), the cell viability values could be affected slightly over the time. The viability of the cells at the concentration of 100 μg/mL was lower than 82% after 48 h. Moreover, the lower cytotoxicity of PTX-loaded nanocarrier compared to free PTX can confirm the effectiveness of the carrier in reducing the cytotoxicity of the drug against normal cells. Fig. 12b shows the results of cytotoxicity effect of drug-loaded nanocarrier on MCF-7 cells. As compared to the nanocomposites alone, paclitaxel-loaded nanocarrier showed a significant reduction in the viability of cells from 85% to 70% that confirmed the effectiveness of the drug components. Furthermore, drug-loaded nanocarrier exhibited a concentration and time-dependent toxicity, so that with increasing the incubation time and concentration, reduction occurred in the viability of the cells. Based on this result, these new types of drug-loaded nanocarrier could be considered a potential candidate for cancer treatment because slow and prolonged release of the drug leads to decreased side effects in body and prevented drug resistance in the cancer cells. The results of cytotoxicity investigations were calculated according to the Tukey test by statistical analysis (SPSS) and the desired values are specified by “*” in the figures.

The hemolysis assay was performed to ensure the excellent blood compatibility of PTX/N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH on human RBCs in the buffer (PBS, pH 7.4) in different concentrations. The hemolysis assay results of both samples demonstrate the hemolytic activity below 3% for all tested concentrations (Fig. 13 ) and no significant hemolytic effect on RBC was detected in the experiment. It is also to be noted that the nanocarrier exhibited lower RBC hemolysis percentages compared to the recent reports [63,64].

Since foreign biomaterials come in contact with blood after being injected into the body, the complement system may be activated as part of the body's immune system, so evaluation of the complement activation in contact with the foreign material is unavoidable [65]. One of the most common approaches to identify the effect of foreign materials on complement activation is the use of complement fragments such as C3 and C4. Herein, the effect of nanocomposites (with/without drug) exposed to the blood plasma on the activation of C3 and C4 was evaluated by using specific kits. The results of complement activation assay demonstrated that both types of samples did not show significant interaction effect with blood components in response to the control. Therefore, the compatibility and safety of these samples are confirmed for using as intravenous drug delivery systems (Fig. 14a). No statistical differences were detected between these samples and controls (p ≤ 0.05)

Blood clotting via coagulation cascade is a type of innate immune system responsive which may activate against a foreign material in the blood that lead to elimination of that material. In fact, coagulation occurs through two main methods; internal pathway and external pathway, through employing several types of factors that finally terminates to the production of fibrin clot. The hemocompatibility of the nanocarrier was evaluated by measuring the time of blood clotting after the addition of prothrombin and thromboplastin (PT and APTT) as factors to evaluate external and internal coagulation pathways. The results (Fig. 14b) confirmed that the nanocarriers (with or without drug) had no effect on activation time of the coagulation pathways and are completely blood compatible. Therefore, it could be confirmed that samples are completely hemocompatible. Statistical analysis of samples was not different from that of the controls (p ≤ 0.05).

In this work, a new multicomponent nanocarrier based on ZnAl-LDH modified with N-GQDs/CoFe2O4 (N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH) was successfully synthesized and designed for a pH-triggered controlled chemotherapeutic delivery. The electrostatic interactions and chemical bonds occurred among components of nanocomposites. This pH-triggered drug delivery system provides a unique platform for PTX delivery owing to the high number of functional groups and specific surface area. Electrochemical techniques, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were performed for proving the surface modification process. Moreover, a series of characterization tests were performed to confirmed drug loading on N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite including FE-SEM, TEM, EDX, DLS, FTIR and XRD, and results showed average size about 171 nm and −38.7 mV surface charge, chemical stability, magnetism feature. Results clearly showed that covering of the nanocarrier surface With polyethylene glycol (PEG) could be constructive in improving the PTX loading capacity, prolonging drug release at neutral pH, toxic reduction, good biocompatibility, high storage stability, high water solubility and targeting capability. It could be created a strong coupling agent between both PTX and N-GQDs for increasing drug-loading capacity. Interestingly, the in vitro drug release profile of the multifunctional nanocarrier of PTX- loaded N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH revealed an ultrahigh encapsulation to 88% and a prolonged release of 22% in comparison to other samples after 3 days in physiological environments (pH 7.4). on the other hand, the most of PTX released in N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite for a short time period at acidic pH (69% for 72 h). As a result, the most PTX in the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite will remain at neutral pH for a considerable period of time (9% after 72 h), affirming the preserved PTX retention time in the blood fluids. This pH-responsive release behavior is desirable for enhancing the cancer treatment efficiency and reducing the side effects. The obtained results of in vitro MTT assay confirmed that the drug-free nanocarrier had no effect on cell viability after incubation with L929 cells at concentrations up to 100 μg/mL, whereas the drug-loaded nanocarrier reduced cancer cell viability by 30% after 72 h, suggesting that the N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite could increase the therapeutic efficacy of PTX. Besides, hemolysis assay showed that drug loaded nanocarrier had no toxicity effect on the red blood cells. Moreover, in vitro hemocompatibility examinations of coagulation and complement activation verified the good blood biocompatibility of nanocarrier even at the high concentrations. Overall, it could be concluded that this novel N-GQDs/CoFe2O4/LDH nanocomposite reveals great potential for the development of new tools towards diagnostic and therapeutic applications.

This work, for the first time, we report a biocompatible drug delivery system for in vitro pH-sensitive drug release based on ZnAl-LDH nanosheets modified CoFe2O4/N-graphene quantum dot for PTX delivery. Ultrahigh PTX encapsulation up to 88% and a slow release behavior (9% after 72 h) under normal physiological conditions were achieved using this nanocarrier. However, releasing ~69% of the drug in a low pH as a model of the extracellular tumor environment happened, presenting a pH-sensitive release behavior. MTT assay was carried out to evaluate cell viabilities of the samples. Up to now, only a few efforts have been devoted to investigation the interaction of PTX-loaded nanocarriers with blood and its constituents. In this work, hemolytic behavior of nanovehicles, coagulation prothrombin time (PT), activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) as blood biocompatibility were applied and these tests revealed the excellent blood compatibility of nanocarrier and complement activation (C3 and C4 levels) showed this functional nanocomposite could be appropriate for biological applications.
